preamble.summary: Esther's second banquet and Haman's downfall. The LXX follows the MT closely. Esther reveals Haman's plot, the king's fury, and Haman is hanged on the gallows he prepared for Mordecai. The reversal theme reaches its climax.
preamble.notable_variants: Minor differences in phrasing. The LXX occasionally expands the emotional descriptions. The core narrative — Esther's revelation, the king's rage, Haman falling on Esther's couch, and execution on his own gallows — is identical.

variant_notes: No significant variant between the LXX and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX and the MT for this verse.

verses.mt_reading: כִּי אֵין הַצָּר שֹׁוֶה בְּנֵזֶק הַמֶּלֶךְ
verses.lxx_reading: οὐ γὰρ ἄξιος ὁ διάβολος τῆς αὐλῆς τοῦ βασιλέως
verses.variant_rendering: for the slanderer is not worthy of the king's court
verses.mt_rendering: for the adversary is not worth the king's loss
variant_notes: The LXX uses diabolos (slanderer/devil) for the MT's tsar (adversary). This Greek word would later become the standard term for Satan in Christian theology.
No significant variant between the LXX and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX and the MT for this verse.

verses.mt_rendering: he had fallen on the couch
variant_notes: Both versions record the dramatic misunderstanding — the king interprets Haman's pleading posture as an assault on Esther. The LXX adds that 'the king was enraged' more emphatically.
Both versions give the height as fifty cubits (roughly 75 feet / 23 meters) — an absurd exaggeration underscoring Haman's overreach.

verses.lxx_reading: καὶ ὁ βασιλεὺς ἐκόπασεν τοῦ θυμοῦ
variant_notes: Both versions note that the king's anger subsided only after Haman's execution. The reversal is complete: the gallows intended for Mordecai receives Haman.
